text.title: GoF Pattern Catalog: Creational, Structural, Behavioral
text.summary: All 23 Gang of Four patterns grouped by category — name, problem solved, and canonical use case. Use this as a lookup table; load the category-specific methodology for implementation details.

rule.statement: Use creational patterns to decouple object creation from the code that uses the objects. Prefer Dependency Injection containers over manual Factory or Singleton implementations in modern frameworks.
rule.rationale: Modern DI frameworks (Spring, FastAPI Depends, NestJS) already implement Factory and Singleton lifecycles. Manual Factory and Singleton add boilerplate without adding control.

list.item: Factory Method: single creation point, concrete type varies at runtime — PaymentProcessor, NotificationService
Abstract Factory: create related object families together — UI toolkit (Windows/Mac), database drivers
Builder: construct a complex object step by step — QueryBuilder, HttpRequest, ConfigBuilder
Prototype: clone an expensive-to-create object — game object spawning, document template cloning
Singleton: guarantee one instance globally — Logger, connection pool (prefer DI scope over manual Singleton)

rule.statement: Distinguish the four wrapping patterns by purpose: Adapter converts an interface, Proxy controls access, Decorator adds behavior, Bridge separates abstraction from implementation before design.
rule.rationale: Teams frequently confuse Adapter, Proxy, and Decorator because all three wrap an object. The selection criterion is the problem being solved, not the structural similarity.

list.item: Adapter: convert interface A to interface B for a legacy or third-party class — payment gateway wrapper
Bridge: independent hierarchies of abstraction and implementation — Notification (Email/SMS) x (Urgent/Normal)
Composite: tree of same-typed nodes, uniform leaf/branch interface — file systems, UI component trees
Decorator: stack cross-cutting behavior dynamically — logging, caching, auth middleware
Facade: single simplified entry point to a complex subsystem — PaymentService hiding card/fraud/ledger
Flyweight: share intrinsic state across thousands of instances — character rendering, game particles
Proxy: control access to an object — lazy loading, caching proxy, access control, remote object stub

rule.statement: Use behavioral patterns to decouple senders from receivers and make algorithms swappable. Prefer composition over class hierarchies for behavioral variation.
rule.rationale: Class-based behavioral variation (switch/if on type) creates coupling and violates OCP. Strategy, Command, and State externalize the varying behavior.

list.item: Strategy: swap algorithms at runtime — payment methods, sorting, pricing rules
Observer: notify multiple listeners of state changes — event systems, pub/sub, reactive UIs
Command: encapsulate a request as an object — undo/redo, job queues, transaction logging
State: change behavior when internal state changes — order lifecycle, media player, workflow engine
Chain of Responsibility: pass request along a handler chain — middleware, validation, approval flows
Template Method: skeleton algorithm with deferred steps — test frameworks, ETL pipelines
Iterator: traverse a collection without exposing its internals — database cursors, pagination
Mediator: centralize interactions between objects — chat rooms, event buses, form orchestration
Visitor: add operations to an object graph without modifying classes — AST processing, report generation
Memento: capture and restore object state — undo mechanisms, save points, snapshots
Interpreter: grammar for a simple language — expression parsers, rule engines (rare in modern systems)
